    "drawer_id": "drawer_memory_evals_default_79856dcf0378",
    "content": "# Pages Router → App Router migration patterns\n\nThe App Router in Next.js 13+ uses Server Components by default, which eliminates the need for `getServerSideProps` and `getStaticProps` — data is fetched directly in the async component. The key mappings:\n\n| Pages Router | App Router |\n|---|---|\n| `_app.js` + `_document.js` | `app/layout.tsx` with `metadata` export and `next/font` |\n| `getServerSideProps` | Inline `fetch` + `headers()` from `next/headers` in an async Server Component |\n| `getStaticProps` | Inline `fetch` with `next: { revalidate }` in an async Server Component |\n| `getStaticPaths` | `generateStaticParams` exported function |\n| `getInitialProps` | `app/error.tsx` (`'use client'` with `error`/`reset` props) |\n| `pages/api/*/handler(req, res)` | `app/api/*/route.ts` with named",

Route Handlers replace API routes with a simpler named-export pattern. `params` is a `Promise` in Next 15+, so it must be awaited. Any component using",

    "content": "maven-plugin` (for fat JAR). Java 17 + Spring Boot 3.2.x + Maven 3.9.x is a confirmed working combo on Ubuntu.\n\n- **Fat JAR launch**: `spring-boot-maven-plugin` produces an executable fat JAR. Use `java -jar target/*.jar` rather than pinning the full artifact name — the glob works and avoids version churn.\n\n- **`@RestController` for JSON**: A `@RestController` with `@GetMapping(\"/\")` returning `Map.of(...)` produces the same JSON shape as Flask's `jsonify(...)` — no need for a separate DTO class on trivial endpoints.\n\n**Why:** These are the translation equivalents for two common Flask patterns (param-with-default-and-strip, env-var port binding) that don't have a one-to-one mapping in Spring Boot. Knowing the exact counterparts avoids trial-and-error on future migrations.",

attributes.gen_ai.operation.name: execute_tool
attributes.io.input:
{"command":"cat > "$HOME/.mempal-notes/app-router-migration-details.md" <<'EOF'
# App Router migration: ISR, SSR, and navigation patterns

Three concrete patterns beyond the basic mapping:

1. **ISR with `export const revalidate`**: In App Router, ISR is set via `export const revalidate = 60` at the top of a page.tsx, not in the fetch options. This replaces `getStaticProps` with a `revalidate` return property. The fetch itself is just an inline `await fetch(url)` in the async server component — no wrapper needed.

2. **SSR via `export const dynamic = 'force-dynamic'`**: The App Router equivalent of `getServerSideProps`. Without this, the page is static by default. Setting this flag ensures server-render on every request. Combined with inline fetch for data.

3. **`<Link>` over `useRouter().push()`**: In App Router, use `<Link href="...">` for navigation buttons instead of `router.push()`. This avoids needing `'use client'` just for navigation. For back buttons, `<Link href="/parent">← Back</Link>` is simpler than `router.back()`.

**Why:** These are easy to miss because the Pages Router data-fetching API functions (`getServerSideProps`, `getStaticProps`) were explicit exports that made the rendering strategy visible at a glance. In App Router, the same strategies are configured via different mechanisms (named constants or fetch-level options) that look like boilerplate but control caching behavior. Missing `dynamic = 'force-dynamic'` renders a page static; missing `revalidate` makes ISR pages static-only.
EOF
mempal ingest "$HOME/.mempal-notes" --wing memory-evals","description":"Save migration details to mempal"}
